title: USDA Plan No. 7201 - 4-Bedroom House with Split-Level Entry (lower & upper level plans)
creator: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service (Cooperative Farm Building Plan Exchange)
rightsholder_or_source: USDA / Internet Archive (contributing institution states item is not in copyright; US Government work)
research_notes: Combined cover sheet (page n2 of the scan) carries both floor plans plus a perspective; USDA Miscellaneous Publication No. 1294, 3-sheet plan set. Page n3 is wall sections (not a plan). Raster page image extracted from the archive.org BookReader.
research_features: Split-level configuration test case: a single sheet drawing BOTH the LOWER LEVEL FLOOR PLAN (family/office-or-bedroom, bath, laundry, two bedrooms) and the UPPER LEVEL FLOOR PLAN (living, dining, kitchen, two bedrooms, bath) with drawn dimensions and a split-level foyer linking the two half-levels. Forces handling of staggered floor levels rather than a single Z-height per floor.
feature_highlights: two staggered half-level plans (lower + upper) on one sheet linked by a split-level foyer
intermediate stair landing between the two half-levels rather than a full story offset
four bedrooms split across the two levels plus family, living, dining, kitchen, two baths, laundry
drawn overall and partial dimensions on each level (~37x16 ft lower, ~38x16 ft upper)
small graphic scale bar under each plan for underlay calibration
perspective sketch plus two distinct floor plans plus marketing prose on a single cover sheet
rectangular rooms with single-swing doors and double-hung windows
supported_examples: rectangular rooms with room labels (family, living, dining, kitchen, bedroom, bath, laundry)
linear point-to-point dimensions in imperial units
roadmap_examples: straight-run stairs as a 2D symbol with floor-spanning topology

gap_features.why: the house is two staggered half-levels offset by a half flight at a shared foyer, not one floor at a single height nor two cleanly stacked full stories; roadmap floor management covers discrete stacked levels but not partial/intermediate ones
